Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic but intense. I work full-time and have limited resources, so I had to squeeze in studying whenever I could—early mornings before work, late nights, weekends. I'd dedicate around 31 hours a week, but it wasn't neatly scheduled. I relied heavily on visual materials like diagrams and charts that I could review quickly. I'd print things out at the library when I could afford it and study from those physical copies a lot.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening at work and what deadlines were coming up. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because of overtime shifts, then I'd have to cram everything into a few intense days. The anxiety of falling behind was constant, honestly. I tried to establish regular times but life just didn't cooperate. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd have my most productive study sessions.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due or when I needed to download materials for offline study. Sometimes I'd get notifications about discussion posts and feel obligated to check in. The fear of missing something important drove a lot of my logins—I was always scared I'd overlook a deadline or announcement. It wasn't really about casual browsing; it was purposeful and often stress-driven.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a comfortable routine for me. I'd log in, grab what I needed, and get out. The peer support group I connected with actually helped more—we'd share summaries and discuss things outside the platform. I guess around midterms I had a brief period where I checked in more regularly, but my low engagement score reflects that I just couldn't sustain that pattern with everything else going on.
Q5: The video lectures were invaluable because I'm a visual learner. I could watch them once, take detailed notes, and then review my notes offline repeatedly. The discussion forums were also helpful when peers shared their understanding of complex topics—that peer support really carried me through some confusing material. Anything visual or diagram-based stuck with me much better than walls of text.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the optional quizzes. With limited internet access at home, I had to prioritize what I downloaded during my library visits. The readings felt less essential when I could get the core concepts from videos and peer discussions. I also delayed a lot of the smaller assignments because I was focused on understanding the material for exams, which is where my score really mattered.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped clarify something I was struggling with. I couldn't afford to spend hours on things that seemed tangential—every minute counted. If I could see how it would improve my understanding or my grade, I was all in. The peer discussions felt valuable because they addressed my actual confusion points in real-time.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones got done because they had to, even if I was exhausted. Optional stuff? I'd look at it and think about all the other things demanding my time and energy. There was guilt around skipping them, but I had to be strategic. My anxiety would spike thinking about all the optional things I wasn't doing.
Q9: Absolutely—most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs at the library, then study from those at home or during breaks at work. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes and diagrams. I also used free YouTube videos and external resources to supplement what the course provided. A lot of my 31 study hours were completely invisible to the LMS.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was intensely studying from printed materials and handwritten notes without touching the LMS at all. I'd be going through flashcards I made, redrawing diagrams from memory, or discussing concepts with my peer support group via text messages. My exam preparation was almost entirely offline—reviewing, self-testing, connecting concepts. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, taking careful notes, and then never watch it again on the platform. But I'd review those notes dozens of times. Same with PDFs—one download, weeks of studying from the printed version. My internet situation made this necessary, but it also worked well for how I learn. I retained more from my handwritten summaries than from rewatching anyway.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially around midterms when work got crazy and my anxiety was through the roof. I felt scared constantly—scared of failing, scared of not keeping up, scared that my limited resources would finally catch up with me. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming. The peer support intervention actually helped pull me through some of those moments.
Q13: Constantly. Fatigue from working full-time made it hard to engage meaningfully with online materials late at night. When I was confused about something, I'd sometimes just close the laptop instead of pushing through because I was too tired to think clearly. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got the worst version of me—rushed, anxious, just trying to check boxes rather than deeply engage.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have the LMS open but I was too mentally exhausted to actually absorb anything. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, or have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely. Those sessions felt hollow—I was physically present but not learning. My high stress levels made it hard to be fully present even when I showed up.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The anxiety was paralyzing sometimes—I'd avoid starting something because I was scared of doing it wrong or not having enough time to do it well. It's contradictory, but the fear of failure made me delay the very things that would prevent failure. I'd focus on exam prep instead, which felt more controllable.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They dictated when I'd have intense bursts of LMS activity versus radio silence. I'd often cram multiple weeks of engagement into the days before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I survived. The deadline pressure actually helped cut through my anxiety sometimes—when there was no choice but to act, I acted.
Q17: Feedback was huge for my motivation. When I got positive feedback, it validated that my unconventional study approach was working despite low visible engagement. Negative or critical feedback would send my anxiety spiraling, making me avoid that area for a while. The peer support helped me process feedback more constructively—having others to talk through comments with made a real difference.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules helped me plan my offline study, but the expectation of regular online participation didn't fit my situation. I appreciated that exams were weighted heavily because that's where I could really demonstrate my learning. The structure assumed more consistent internet access and time than I had available.
Q19: The video content being downloadable was a lifesaver—that made engagement possible for me. But the discussion post requirements were harder because they demanded real-time participation I couldn't always manage. Timed activities or things requiring immediate responses were stressful. Anything I could engage with on my own schedule worked better for my circumstances.
Q20: I think that could reflect genuine confusion or struggling to understand what's expected. Someone might be searching desperately for clarity, watching videos multiple times, reading everything, but still not feeling confident enough to submit work. Or they could be dealing with anxiety like me—engaging with materials but paralyzed when it comes to producing something that will be judged.
Q21: That's basically my story. My attendance and LMS engagement numbers are embarrassingly low, but I got a Distinction. I studied intensely—just not in ways the system could see. I focused on deep understanding rather than surface-level participation. It shows that engagement isn't just about clicks and logins; it's about what's happening in your mind and your notebooks.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and timing pretty accurately—when someone logs in, what they click, how long pages are open. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and everything happening offline. They can't see the hours I spent with printed notes, the peer conversations, the mental wrestling with concepts. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, connecting new concepts to what I already know, and caring about actually understanding—not just passing. It's an internal state of curiosity and effort, even when it's mixed with fear and stress. For me, engagement looked like filling notebooks and having late-night discussions with peers, not clicking through modules.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I fought to be here, how much anxiety I pushed through, or how my peer support network literally kept me going. It wouldn't show the pride I felt when concepts finally clicked after hours of offline review. It can't capture that I'm a 27-year-old working full-time with limited resources who still managed a Distinction through sheer determination and strategic studying.
